author: depressedcubic
title: Who in their fucking right mind would force ppl to vibe code their assignemtn
comment_content: 2/3 unsupervised learning + 1/3 LLM yapping because He fucking loves LLM
comment_teaching:
Notes is about as organized as an pile of newspapers cutout.

He is good at explaining the intuition as well as providing some of his own insight into the topics, but to dig deep into how to actually do any of the things or what any of the things actually means on a somewhat rigorous level requires a lot more digging.

I would've given this a B if not for how much I fucking hate the programming assignment
comment_grading:
obviously not yet out, later fill.

also fuck attendance.
comment_workload:
Programming Assignment.... but only use Claude Code.

Those retarded UST Sponsor some calls taught PG students might be very happy seeing that they can vibe their assignment legitimately, but this is such a fucking terrible experience that i have to write this fucking review early to talk about this shit.

Those who have taken 4901B last semester might have used the DeepSeek API key provided by He to the whole course for the project. As He really wants to promote his sloppy trash dream of everyone presumably having gay sex with LLM, he have forced the usage of Claude Code with this DeepSeek API key (not the same one from the past ofc) for both fairness and accessibility.

Like how he found the 2080Ti cluster for the assignments in last semesters 4901B, this sounds like a great idea on paper, but falls apart drastically once you spent a nanosecond trying to actually think and do the thing.

The deepseek model might be decent at coding in small and isolated instance as snippets, but as a backbone for an agentic tool it falls apart so rapidly, sometimes failing to run a fucking command multiple times and hallucinate + forget context very readily.

Now, for a regular person with an IDE on the side, this is fine as they could just type their code merrily and have deepshit autocomplete stuff as they go. But He have the wonderful idea of forcing 100% usage of claude code in this assignment (THEY GRADE THE FUCKING CLAUDE CODE LOG). This means, you have to look that the fucker write shit over and over again while whipping his ass to make sure it is writing sensible stuff.

Now, to that one motherfucker who think he is slick with his vibe coded garbage slop and believe that I am simply "bad at vibe coding". Please note that by the time I write for the 10th times that long ass spec of exactly what i wanted and waited for the deepseek server to pump back token at the speed of a llm running on my fucking laptop, I would've produced much better and maintainable code following exactly what I want while getting more familiar with the machine learning frameworks and getting more satisfaction out of this whole ordeal.

Like even if you just encouraged use of claude code while allowing a workflow that is mainly typing code like a normal person without mental retardation it would be totally fine, but you chose to force 100% claude code. So now, I am looking at a sloppy garbage codebase that throws a hundred warnings with my strict lsp setup. There is no human way to fix this level of mess, and to guide it properly requires the mental effort (and time thanks to slow server) of just writing the fucking code by hand but not getting the satisfaction of writing the code yourself. Then its output is of course 500 lines of pure trash that is so mentally straining to review and time-consuming to wait for that half lobotomized toddler to do what i want that i might as well write the fucking code myself 3 times over while ending up with code that is way nicer to work with.

Like, I get it, for well structured code, it is really nice to just have it throw up some small snippets and have the ai tools to put it in place nicely immediately. But for a LLM (espeically a fuck ass weak one) to fully autonomously handle a project and produce code is just such a fucking nonsensical thing that I imagine only PGTA (or more accurately GPTA) under He's supervision will use while producing programming assignment that do API requests in a blocking manner. Especially in the near future after the AI bubble pops, such workflow relying purely on a strong base model will only yield worse result. Granted it is really nice to have an intelligent autocomplete for speeding up code writing, but any attempt at having it to do full agentic coding is simply a performative action to pretend that you have used advanced AI tools instead of using the time that you used for your dumb prompt engineering to actually type more code and get better at coding. Anyone who believe that this is a good idea at promoting vibe coding have fundamentally misunderstood the strength of LLM and lacks basic experience at writing code that is even slightly complex.

Before you dislike this review because you are very accustomed to this kind of vibe coding workflow, please note that deepseek is just a much worse model at coding so you will likely have a much more terrible time using it instead of using the fine-tuned and strong Claude opussy 4.6 or gpt 5.2 codsex models or whatever the fuck the big AI corpo shits out next that have 0.1% performance increase that you use. And trust me, I have experienced how much an LLM can speed up development, but to babysit this kind of shitty model requires such a drastic workflow shift that have little to no (and perhaps negative) gain is just really not my taste. If you think it is reasonable to do this kind of workflow shift for a single dumb fuck assignment that only helps you have miniscule gain on your productivity, please imagine COMP 2011/2 forcing you to learn vim for doing one of its assignment (tho learning and getting accustomed to vim is way more valuable than learning a suboptimal workflow that gets even worse once the AI bubble pops imo). Again, I agree with the intention for encouraging the exploration of AI tools usage as learning new things is essentially what a student should be doing anyway. But this is just the wrong way to both educate and to code. This unreasonable AI hype is honestly just poisoning UST and I hope no other course will pull any similar thing ever again.

TLDR; FUCK VIBE CODING

author: Henry_111
title: COMP 5212 Review
comment_content:
The content covers most of the tranditional machine learning models and goes deep into them, providing you with a comprehensive understanding of the details behind. This courses is more difficult than previous machine learning courses but you can definitely learn a lot from it.
Basically it covers Linear Regression, Logistic regression, Exponential Family, Generalized linear models, Kernel Methods,Kernel methods, SVM,Generative Models,Naive Bayes, MLE, MAP,Generalization, bias-variance tradeoff,Clustering, EM,PCA before midterm.

And Probabilistic Graphical Models,HMM,Neural Networks, backpropagation,Transformer, Variational autoencoder,GANs, Reinforcement Learning, Language models after midterm.

This courses covers much topics while each topic may worth several hours to teach. So it will be a bit rush, but professor tried to deliver all the key points to you in limited time.
comment_teaching: Professor He's teaching is really good. He can explain all the content clearly and in details. The most important thing is that he will tell you his own understanding about the stuff.  However, the content is too much, if you want to make everything clear, you may still need to take some effort after class.
comment_grading:
The professor said the grading will be generous.

And homework has some bonus questions for us to improve the grade. If you master the content in class, you can answer the bonus questions.
comment_workload:
In total there are 4 written homework + 1 coding assignment + midterm exam + final exam.
The 4 written homework helps us review the content in class and extend a bit to learn the topic deeper. It takes some time to do them but it worth spend time.

The professor said the exam will be much easier than homework.

author: xklong
comment_content: his course encompasses a wide range of machine learning topics, providing you with a comprehensive understanding of the underlying theory. By completing this course, you will acquire a solid foundation in machine learning.
comment_teaching: Due to the extensive coverage of topics in this course, the teaching pace may be relatively fast.
comment_grading: The examination for this course is conducted with closed books, meaning you won't have access to external references. However, the exam itself is not overly challenging. By diligently following the guidance of the instructor and thoroughly reviewing the lecture notes, you can expect to achieve a high grade.
comment_workload: The workload is a little bit heavy. It requires 4 written assignments first. I choose not to present so I have to finish 6 hands-on assignments as well.

author: Ktickop
comment_content: The contents are comprehensive, covering (1) standard machine learning topics like linear regression, logistic regression, and simple generative models (Naive Bayes), Bias-variance trade-off;  (2) Deep learning models like FFN, CNN, RNN, and Transformer; (3) Deep generative models like VAE, GAN, diffusion models; (4) Reinforcement learning. Each parts are accompanied by hands-on or written assignments to deepen your understanding. So, after studying this course, you should have a good command of basic ideas of machine learning and deep learning. The course is taught using a probabilistic perspective, so a good understanding of probability theory would be beneficial.
comment_teaching: The teaching is good and the explanation is intuitive. The teaching materials and plan are well-designed. The instructor has posted recorded videos that you could watch after class. Also, he is available and responsive on Piazza to answer questions by himself.
comment_grading: Though the final exam is open-book and you may bring up to four cheat sheets, I found the questions not trivial; you need to have a good understanding of the materials covered in the class in order to answer the questions and get high grades. The final exam consists of 64% of the final grade, which I think is a little bit large.
comment_workload: The workload is substantial, comprising 4 written assignments and approximately 5 hands-on assignments to complete. A similarity score exceeding 40% will give you a penalty on the grade, so you need to be careful to answer the questions in the assignment.

author: Jacklii
title: COMP5212 This is a very good course (but challenging)
comment_content: It covers almost all machine learning models, such as bert, transformer diffusion, cnn, rnn, lstm, etc., and reasoning all the formula foundations and model foundations in detail. You need to follow him carefully in the early stage of each class; otherwise, you will understand more and more. (Utilitarianism speaking,) There is no need to attend this course in class. The ppt covers a very wide range of derivations, and the teacher will complete the derivations in great detail. However, most of the questions in the exam are very fragmented explanation questions. You need to ensure that you can basically cover all the knowledge content. Derivations are a "super bonus point" for the exam.
comment_teaching: A very, very, very, very, very, very, very responsible teacher. The lesson preparation is completely serious, and the homework is strictly formulated according to the teaching schedule. Listening to his classes is a kind of enjoyment
comment_grading: The scoring for regular homework is not very lenient. About 10 assignments are given a regular score of around 40
comment_workload: The workload is heavy, with 4 WA and more than 5 HA. The HA assignments are not difficult, but some of the WA questions can be quite challenging. Since it's an electronic assignment, and considering the time spent on typing formulas, some of the proof questions in WA can be postponed for about two hours. I think it's highly likely that I won't be able to solve them during the exam. Sure, part of the reason is that my foundation in mathematics and science is not good enough. Surprisingly, the connection between the exam and the regular homework is very small. The types of questions in the regular homework account for less than 10% of the exam and are almost never included. Even if they are included, the content is extracted from a single homework question and condensed into a 2-point short question, There are approximately three sub-questions. 2+2+5=9 points is included in the homework.

author: seeker614
title: Good course
comment_content: All theoretical things you need to know about machine learning.
comment_teaching: Very good teaching with both examples and theoretical inference. The professor takes courses from cmu and Stanford as reference.
comment_grading: The professor is generous when giving grades. I got more than B+.
comment_workload: A little bit large. The first assignment  took me 14 hours, and the second 6 hours, the third 8 hours. The fourth is en exercise before exam. There’s also a project, an easy midterm exam and a little bit hard final exam. But in all, the assignments are more difficult than the exams but discussions with classmates is permitted.

author: hkustcsepg
comment_content: This course includes valuable information on machine learning topics such as probability, regression, RNN, CNN, Transformer, GAN, VAE, and RL. It is highly beneficial and applicable.
comment_teaching:
Nevin is an excellent teacher, and his courses are straightforward to understand. However, he did mention in the first class that his course is not suitable for students without prior knowledge of machine learning. Nonetheless, I believe it could be beneficial for beginners who are willing to put in the effort to learn.
And I think his website about the course is quite clear for students with a lot of useful information about course info, announcements, course materials and RVC videos.
comment_grading:
4 Written Assignments: (4x5%)
Projects: (30%)
Final examination: (50%)
I received a score of 5/5 on most of my homework assignments.
My performance on the final exam was close to the average score but a little higher.
That's A-
comment_workload: I find the workload a bit burdensome. The homework and coding labs require a significant amount of my time almost every week. However, Nevin presented an alternative - an opportunity to work on a machine learning project. Those who choose to take on the team project(2 to 3 students as a team) can avoid completing the ongoing coding labs.

author: N4Y
comment_content: This course covers a wide range of topics in Machine Learning. It also introduces new research in the relevant areas. The main focus of the course is the theoretical foundation behind all the ML techniques, which means you need to understand the equations and algorithms thoroughly.
comment_teaching: Prof. ZHANG's course materials are well-prepared. Basically all the questions in the final exam are covered in the course material. He also provides the recordings of the lecture which are useful for revision.
comment_grading: Since this is a PG course the average grading is relatively higher. Around 50% of all students can get A range. The evaluate criteria includes written assignments, programming project/assignments, and a final exam. Students can choose from complete a group project or finish multiple programming assignments. The written assignments are not very hard and the contents are covered in the course material.
comment_workload: The workload is reasonable. All the homework are not very time-consuming. If you have experience in writing research papers, the programming assignment should not be very challenging since there are many existing code online that can be reused and modified.

author: PeterGam
title: Boring class with unclear explaination
comment_content: At a high level, this course can be splitted into two modules: fundamentals and recent researches. The fundamentals cover some mathematic basics (e.g., derivatives, matrix calculation, probability theory) and why machine learning works from theory level. This part is derived from UCLA CS 260. The other part focuses on recent advances in deep learning (e.g., transformers, GANs, semi-supervised learning).
comment_teaching: Unsatisfactory. It can be observed that the instructor tries to make things clear. However, most of the time he would waste on the simple concepts and nearly skipped on what's unfamiliar to me (e.g., graph theory). Even some times the instructor receive no response for the question "If someone understands, please raise your hand", leaving an awkward classroom atmosphere.
comment_grading: I think it is like a binary grading. The final is not difficult. For assignments, as long as you can "complete" them, there will be full marks (since the expected outcome for programming and proofs is obvious), otherwise the marks would be low.
comment_workload: The workload can also be splitted into two categories. For programming projects, you may Google it or search Github for the original answers (as I've said in the content section, it basically copies UCLA CS260). It contains just some simple prediction algorithms. However, for the written homework, it would cost a lot of efforts to dig into it since it is proofs to some of mathematical concepts with few reference you can refer to.

author: NotSaitama
title: Below average teaching and grading
comment_content: The topics in the syllabus were quite interesting. Few topics covered are Theory of generalisation, SVM, DNN, etc.
comment_teaching: He partially copied his slides from the course by Prof. Yaser (Caltech), but was not very good at explaining them. I also found it hard to understand what he is speaking as he speaks in a very low voice.
comment_grading:
No consideration was given to whether the student understands the topic, but only whether the answer is ditto copy of what the instructor wishes to see there. Using any other valid definition than what was presented will lead to deduction in marks.  

No explanation is usually given on many assignments like why they deduct marks until you ask explicitly, and even then your request could be ignored indefinitely.

I also found that he himself doesn't understand many concepts, and is extremely hostile toward any sort of discussion about grading.
This was the first course of his career, and I hope he will becomes a better teacher in the future.
comment_workload: Not much workload. Few assignments, one project, and one exam. The instructor never told or have any useful feedback on the project proposal, but was very quick to criticize the final report. I would have preferred if he has given some feedback earlier so we can improve.

author: mufan
title: Machine Learning
comment_content:
Part 0:Introduction and Preparation
Basics of Probability/Information Theory
Part 1:Foundation of Machine Learning0
Linear and Polynomial Regression
Logistic and Softmax Regression
Generative Models for Classification
o Learning Theory
Part 2: Foundation of Deep Learning
Feedforward Neural Networks, Backpropagation, and Optimization
Convolutional Neural Networks and Residual Connections
Recurrent Neural Networks and Attention
Part 3: Introduction to Advanced Deep Learning
Introduction to Transformers, BERT and GPT
Introduction to Vision Transformers and Vision-Language Models
Part 4: Generative Models for lmages
o Introduction to Variational autoencoders
o Introduction to Generative adversarial networks
o Introduction to Diffusion Models and Flow Models
Part 5: Introduction to Reinforcement Learning
o Introduction to RL
Introduction to Value-Based Deep Rl
o Introduction to Policy-Based Deep Rl

No attendance.
comment_teaching:
Good.
The teacher's lectures were very clear, but the overall s peaking pace was rather slow. Also, the content contained too many mathematical derivations (which wouldn't be covered in the exam), making it difficult to keep up.
comment_grading:
Good.
Most of questions in the final exam are theoretical and comprehension-based, with few requiring calculations.
The exam allows you to bring a cheat sheet, which can reduce some of the workload and difficulty.
comment_workload:
Heavy.
Both hands-on assignment (coding) and written assignment (theoretical quiz and calculations). Written homework needs you to use Latex.

With the assistance of AI, it's not that terrible.

author: realalanc
comment_content:
A little bit difference from previous version: a kaggle competition, so it needs you to be able to program
With useful cutting edge methods, better than the similar-named class, I think
comment_teaching:
You need to review it from time to time
Her explanation on math formula parts is not so good, so prepare yourself with GPT, lol
comment_grading:
hw+kaggle part + attendance + mid +final
Very good
attendance only be counted once, make yourself a lucky man
Don't worry too much about your exam, you can get an A even you leave 10 marks for blank both in the mid and final exam
comment_workload:
It's OK if you can handle with the kaggle part
WARNING the exam will be broader and harder than hw, do not rely on hw too much

author: ym1111
comment_content:
Very comprehensive ML lecture
Not so easy to understand without strong mathematical knowledge

Introduction
Math basics
Linear Regression
Logistic regression, Exponential Family
Generalized linear models, Kernel Methods
Kernel methods
SVM
Generative Model
Naive Bayes, MLE, MAP
Generalization, bias-variance tradeoff
Clustering, EM
Expectation Maximization
PCA
mid-term exam
Probabilistic Graphical Models
HMM
HW2 Due
Programming HW Out
HMM
Neural Networks, backpropagation
Neural architectures
Transformer, Variational autoencoder
Variational autoencoder Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
GANs, Reinforcement Learning
Generative Adversarial Networks
Reinforcement Learning
Language models, pretraining
Large language models
comment_teaching: Clear and well-designed
comment_grading: I did not fully understand all the contents, but I got a nice grade
comment_workload:
The workload is a little bit large for PG students

There are 3 homework and a programming project

author: hosh1noAI
comment_content: sufficient knowledge about ml, u can learn base practice from this course, but contents are a little bit difficult to learn so quick.
comment_teaching: A little bit fast in pace, not easy to follow but u are welcome to ask questions after class, Fung May are kind
comment_grading: It’s fair graded and great graded on assignments, The TA are responsible for hw, and I am waiting for my exam results to judge this part later
comment_workload: Not too much so just some assignments, and quizzes for attendance ,the accuracy doesn’t matter

author: serein
comment_content: The course mainly covers supervised learning (like regression, classification, SVMs, Naive Bayes), unsupervised learning (clustering, EM, HMMs), optimization and inference basics (gradient descent, MLE, MAP), and then moves into deep learning (neural networks, VAEs, GANs), finishing with an introduction to large language models.
comment_teaching: The professor speaks at quite a fast pace, which can be a bit challenging to follow at times.
comment_grading:
Attendance – 10%
- Recorded through occasional in-class quizzes.
- Achieving 80% attendance earns full marks (e.g., if there are 10 quizzes, you can miss 2 and still get full score).
- Quiz correctness does not affect the grade.

Assignments – 50%
- 4 written + optional programming assignments: 9% each (total 36%)
- 1 programming-only assignment (Kaggle competition style): 14%

Mid-term Exam – 20%
- 1 A4 double-sided cheatsheet allowed.

Final Exam – 20%
- Midterm cheatsheet + 1 additional A4 double-sided cheatsheet allowed.

author: junle
title: Machine Learning
comment_content: The course mainly covers supervised learning (like regression, classification, SVMs, Naive Bayes), unsupervised learning (clustering, EM, HMMs), optimization and inference basics (gradient descent, MLE, MAP), and then moves into deep learning (neural networks, VAEs, GANs), finishing with an introduction to large language models. For details, you can refer to https://mayrfung.github.io/teaching/comp5212f25.
comment_teaching: The professor speaks at quite a fast pace, which can be a bit challenging to follow at times, but since I already have some background in machine learning, I find it manageable. The professor is very good and tries best to make the students understand the knowledge points.
comment_grading:
Attendance – 10%
- Recorded through occasional in-class quizzes.
- Achieving 80% attendance earns full marks (e.g., if there are 10 quizzes, you can miss 2 and still get full score).
- Quiz correctness does not affect the grade.

Assignments – 50%
- 4 written + optional programming assignments: 9% each (total 36%)
- 1 programming-only assignment (Kaggle competition style): 14%

Mid-term Exam – 20%
- 1 A4 double-sided cheatsheet allowed.
Final Exam – 20%
- 4 A4 double-sided cheatsheet allowed.
---------
updated: very good I think. It's easy to get full marks for homework. The exam score is also higher than expected.
comment_workload:
The workload includes regular attendance checks, four written assignments with optional coding, and one larger programming assignment in a Kaggle style. In addition, there is a mid-term and a final exam, both open-book with limited cheatsheets. However, the assignments and kaggle competition are not difficult.

The assignments account for 60% of the grade, so you do not need to worry about the grade of the examination since the grading is very lenient, and both the midterm and final exams only account for 20% each.

author: DanielC
title: Great content but challenging materials
comment_content:
Very good content but supper difficult for non-CS students. I am from business side and it's extremely challanging for me even I have background in required level.
But the content is organized very well. I actually did better in my final than in mid-term exam although final is harder. I got B+ in the end by finishing all assignments, quiz, Kaggle competition, and exams. It's challanging but very worthwhile.
comment_teaching: Dr. He Junxian is a wonderful teacher. He explains things very well and organized. And he is wiiling to esplain dum questions for business students like me.
comment_grading: I have to say, the grading is very lenient. I am a beginner of the class materials. Sometimes I only partially understand the materials. But when I tried my best to finish all the assignments, the grading of my assignments are very high. The instruction team was willing to let everyone get a B or B+ grade. So just try your best to do the homework.
comment_workload: Extremely large workload for me, expecially when I only had limited time. But the evaluation was very lenient.

author: Faye429
title: excellent, but the workload is heavy
comment_content: Covers almost all machine learning models, bert transformer diffusion cnn rnn lstm,rl etc. and reasons in detail about all the formula bases and model bases, you need to follow him carefully in the lead up to each session or you'll get more and more confused.
comment_teaching: Very responsible! I think he is one of the most responsible teachers I've ever met. He writes and updates his notes on the course website, and he works out a lot of formulas on the spot. I think he's one of the most responsible teachers I've ever had. He's also very lively and gives lots of examples.
comment_grading: I don't think the grading is very reasonable, of course it could be my personal reasons. 4 handwritten assignments + 5-6 programming assignments ended up with less than 40% of the grade, only 4% each time. And because of the similarity check, it is often necessary to avoid the use of formulas in the assignments.
comment_workload: I don't think the workload is very reasonable, of course it could be my personal reasons. 4 handwritten assignments + 5-6 programming assignments ended up with less than 40% of the grade, only 4% each time.

author: Milverton
comment_content: From basic ml to cnn rnn lstm to transformer bert Some rl is also covered。
comment_teaching: The professor was exceptionally detailed and the class covered tons and tons of formulas for hand-pushing.Teachers are very good at teaching and very enthusiastic.
comment_grading: 8 assignments + final exam, the exam allows four cheatsheets with conceptual questions for a more comprehensive examination.
comment_workload: There are also multiple assignments and programming homework assignments in class, as well as a number of Tutorials classes and extracurricular material to read, which are notoriously difficult in terms of both quantity and depth. There are also Term Projects waiting for you, projects that cover a lot of self-study and code work, and are encouraged to be novel and more challenging.

author: gym
comment_content: The course content is quite comprehensive and requires some prerequisites. If you do not have a background in linear algebra, you will need to spend some time studying it before taking the course.
comment_teaching: The lecture was delivered quite seriously; however, I feel that it did not connect well with the exam topics. Additionally, there aren't many reference exercises for the assignments, which may lead to a disconnect with the exam content. Of course, this may not be an issue for those who already have a strong background in calculations.
comment_grading: The grading is very generous. I originally thought I was going to fail, but the teacher still passed me.
comment_workload: The workload is not heavy; mainly, it's important to pay attention during the lectures

author: cvxbzn
comment_content: Course content includes regression, SVM, bayes, EM algorithm, HMM and some VAE & GAN. The professor published the slides here. https://jxhe.github.io/teaching/comp5212f24. This course is similar to the one in CMU, so quality of content can be guaranteed.
comment_teaching: The teaching is rather comprehensive, if you have some previous knowledge, it is quite easy to understand.
comment_grading: Good. Almost everyone gets grade above B. However, note that many students get the bonus, it is still competitive and shall be taken seriously.
comment_workload: Not easy. Writing assignments require a thorough understanding of the knowledge, especially in SVM and EM algorithm, while the kaggle competition may be kind of tricky.

author: nickname_
comment_content: This course covers foundational ML concepts and introduces advanced topics, including linear regression, exponential family, GLM, SVM, generative models, MLE, MAP, EM, HMM, VAE, and GAN.
comment_teaching: The teaching is effective, with a focus on math, including probability formula derivations and proofs. However, the instructor occasionally skips some details, which might be confusing for certain students.
comment_grading: There would be 95% B range according to the course introduction lecture.
comment_workload: The workload is somewhat heavy, requiring students to complete four written assignments and one Kaggle project. The private leaderboard for the Kaggle project is only released twice, so it’s important not to miss it to avoid overfitting.

author: suxi
comment_content: This course covers key machine learning concepts, from foundational algorithms like linear and logistic regression to advanced topics such as SVMs, generative models, EM, PCA, and probabilistic graphical models. It explores neural networks, modern architectures like transformers and VAEs, and emerging areas like GANs, RL, and large language models.
comment_teaching: Prof. HE is good at teaching.
comment_grading:
20% Mid-term exam
20% Final exam
50% assignments and project
10% Attendance
If you finsih all assignments and not very bad in two exams, you can easily pass this course.
comment_workload: The workload is not small. Student need to finish 4 assignment and 1 kaggle project.

author: Estella2002
comment_content: 课程讲了传统的机器学习以及一些深度学习的算法。比较重要的课程是svm，hmm等。对了，需要考勤，4/5给满分
comment_teaching: 老师的讲解很深入，对线性代数和概率论需要有一定的背景才能懂得公式。老师讲解的时候，会讲的很深入，可以在原理上对机器学习有进一步的理解。
comment_grading: 给分目前暂不确定，还没出分呢。考勤占比10%，作业4次36%，project14%，半期考20%，期末20%
comment_workload: 四次作业，一次project，在kaggle上面，做一个简单的线性回归，算MSE，来进行排行，这个project需要达到baseline会比较好，四次的作业还挺难的

author: stanly
comment_content: Enough, covers most of the necessary techniques. And these are quite inspiring and helped me with my academic research.
comment_teaching: The new professor, so not as good as an experienced one. But overall, he is trying his best to give us a good lesson, I really understand it is not easy for a new professor.
comment_grading: Good, very fair. And I like this course and the instructor style.
comment_workload: a little time-consuming. A lot of maths about the ground theory, we can understand that machine learning itself is not a black box. the problem is only from deep learning.

author: Shelton
comment_content:
Introduction to Machine Learning
Math basics: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability
Linear models: Linear Regression, Logistic Regression and SVM
Convex Optimization: Smoothness, Lasso Regression, Gradient descent, stochastic gradient descent and its variants
Kernel methods
Tree-based method
Deep Learning:
Convolutional Neural Networks
Recurrent Neural Networks
Transformer and NLP
His research field in Machine Learning
AutoML
Trustworthy Machine Learning
comment_teaching: The content of the course has little to do with the assignment, written assignments are related to convex optimization.
comment_grading: Grading is good.
comment_workload:
3(written)+2(programming) assignment
written assignment: derivation, convex, smoothness, hessian matrix, Newton method, lasso regression
programming assignment: CNN, LSTM

author: zhengrui.guo
comment_content: The contents are quite comprehensive and the instructor covered almost every aspect in Machine Learning.
comment_teaching: I think the teaching part can be further improved. Dr. Cheng definitely knows what he's talking about but sometimes it's hard to interpret to students. Maybe he can talk louder next time.
comment_grading: You can definitely get high grade as long as you go through the slides thoroughly and listen to the recordings. The first two assignments are quite challenging since they are all mathematical problems. But the following ones become easier with programming problems.
comment_workload: The workload was fine. There are 5 assignments with mathematical problems and programming problems. Each may take around 2-3 days. There are no midterm. And you need to conduct a machine learning-related project and do the presentation. And there is a final.

author: ust_pg
comment_content: The course contents are nicely designed, covering both theoretical foundations and high-level ideas. The focus of the course is on deep learning techniques such as CNN, RNN, Transformer, VAE, and GAN, as opposed to traditional machine learning algorithms. Brief introductions to algorithms in recent papers are also provided for each topic. It is recommended that students have some basic knowledge of machine learning before taking this course as many topics are covered. The course provides helpful tutorials that teach about programming and hands-on homework. Programming homework gives students the skeleton code, so students know where to start.
comment_teaching: Prof. Zhang is an excellent teacher who provides wonderful intuitions in this course. He delivers the core concepts in more detail and uses a tablet to write notes, which is helpful for students to follow. The course also utilizes Piazza to facilitate question-asking. In addition, the TAs also provide great support to the students. Overall, the course is well-organized and provides a great learning experience.
comment_grading: The final grade for the course is determined by the final exam, which accounts for 50% of the total score, homework, which accounts for 20%, and the project, which accounts for 30%. For the project, students have the option to complete multiple programming assignments or work on a team project related to their chosen topic. The final exam is a reflection of the amount of effort put into the course.
comment_workload: Completing the homework and project assignments for this course can cost quite some time, but the good thing is that you can learn from the well-designed materials. The most time-consuming aspect of the course is reviewing the class materials because of the packed course contents.

author: moonlightstar
title: A useful and exciting machine learning course to take!
comment_content: Course are useful, well prepared, and could be considered as complete deep introductory materials to provide mathematical and implementation know-how of fundamental progression in machine learning.
comment_teaching: Prof. Zhang explained the materials very well. His knowledge is so deep and he went as far as giving an accurate analogy to illustrate his point, and make sure that his points are delivered well to the students.
comment_grading: The grading is generous. 50% will get an A range, and 90% will get a minimum B, I think, but the Prof notes that we are working hard also for other things, so he tries not to make us worried too much about the final exam.
comment_workload: HWs and HAs can be too much for people without basic math and Python, but I reckon the workload is still doable even for people with such conditions. I didn't take the project path, so I can't comment on that.

author: Yisen Gao
comment_content: In the first half of the semester, we will cover traditional machine learning. In the second half, we will discuss the content of deep learning, including transformer, CNN, and reinforcement learning.
comment_teaching: t's good, but for those without prior knowledge, they might not be able to fully understand.
comment_grading: The score is very good. The assignment is not difficult and can be completed with a perfect score. The mid-term exam was a bit challenging, but it accounted for a relatively small proportion.
comment_workload: The assignment will be completed soon. The final project might take 1 to 2 days to complete. Therefore, the workload is very small.

author: Seanthesheep
comment_content: The course content primarily covers the fundamental principles of machine learning, with a significant portion dedicated to statistics and linear algebra. Additionally, it explores the underlying principles of Transformers.
comment_teaching: Junxian is a highly knowledgeable instructor who clearly possesses a deep understanding of the subject matter. His insights on VAE are particularly insightful (though I must admit the course is a bit challenging for me).
comment_grading: This course is graded fairly leniently. I did my best to complete all the assignments, which were somewhat challenging for me and took a lot of time. If you have a solid grasp of the prerequisite math concepts, it shouldn't take too long. My midterm and final exam scores were average, but after the comprehensive assignments and project, I ended up with a B+.
comment_workload: This isn't an easy course. In fact, it consumed a significant amount of my time—averaging an entire day each week just to complete the assignments—while constantly worrying about failing. This is particularly painful for a PG student who must devote most of their energy to balancing research and coursework.

author: Vayne
comment_content:
Well Organized, up-to-date content. Nevin updated the content every year actually.

Little programming thing is involved in the lecture; most of the things are about the theory side (but useful).
comment_teaching: Clear enough; easy to follow with a lot of handwriting maths.
comment_grading: Fair enough for most students. Not difficult to get an A for a PG student.
comment_workload:
Okay for me. But some students complain the workload is too high with the writing assignments, program assignments, the project, and the final exam.

The questions in the final exam are similar to those in the writing assignment.

author: yyj
title: Review
comment_content: The course covers basic concepts of machine learning. The contents are easy to follow and useful.
comment_teaching: The professor and TA are good at teaching and their delivery is really nice.
comment_grading: The professor is very generous. It's easy to get B+ as long as you follow the workload and your exam score is not too low.
comment_workload: The workload is not too light, but not too heavy. Assignments and final exam are not too hard while the project is quite time consuming and hard to get a great score.

author: Leric
title: Vert great
comment_content: The content is very useful I think. It is fundamental for all PG students, not only people who work in the machine learning area.
comment_teaching: Nevin Zhang teaches very clearly on both the overall picture and the details. I like him very much.
comment_grading: Good grade, easy to get A or above even for me. I think if you want to promote your GPA, then you should absolutely choose this course.
comment_workload: Very little workload. It is easy to finish the homework and projects. If you do not want to cost too much time, please choose this.

author: CatherineChen
comment_content: Covered many basic aspects of ML, from basic calculations such as backwardpropagation and gradient descent to specific structures such as CNN, LSTM, GAN.
comment_teaching: Nevin is really a good lecturer. He prepared slides carefully, maintained a good pace in class, and answered questions in time.
comment_grading: Grading seems good as far as I am concerned.
comment_workload: Not too much. Written assignments are basic, if you follow the lectures. You need to work on a group project, but you can choose virtually any topic related to ML. Final exam is not so hard, either.

author: CleverGao
comment_content: The content encompasses a comprehensive coverage of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Support Vector Machines (SVMs), spanning both cutting-edge advanced methodologies and classic traditional techniques in the field. It not only delves into the state-of-the-art architectures, optimization strategies, and real-world applications of LLMs—representing the forefront of modern artificial intelligence research—but also revisits the foundational principles, mathematical underpinnings, and time-tested use cases of SVMs, a cornerstone of traditional machine learning. By integrating these two distinct yet complementary paradigms, the content offers a holistic perspective on the evolution and practical implications of machine learning technologies.
comment_teaching: teaching can be better
comment_grading: high
comment_workload: not too high

author: lalala0211
comment_content: This course encompasses a diverse range of topics, each of which could potentially necessitate several hours of in-depth instruction. Consequently, the pace may seem somewhat hurried as the professor endeavors to impart the essential knowledge and key points within the allotted time frame. However, efforts have been made to ensure that the core concepts are communicated effectively despite the compressed timeframe.
comment_teaching: Professor He's teaching is commendable, as he possesses the ability to elucidate all the course materials with clarity and thoroughness. What sets him apart is his willingness to share his personal insights and perspectives, enhancing students' understanding. Nevertheless, due to the extensive nature of the content, achieving a comprehensive grasp may necessitate additional effort outside of class.
comment_grading: nice
comment_workload: The coursework comprises a total of four written homework assignments, one coding assignment, a midterm exam, and a final exam. These written homework assignments serve as a valuable tool for reinforcing class content and fostering deeper understanding of the topics covered. Although they require a dedicated time investment, the effort expended is well worth it in terms of enhancing learning outcomes.

author: hap
title: Good
comment_content:
The topic includes basic machine learning concepts, linear regression, generalization, RL concepts, generative models, unsupervised learning, etc.

The lecture discusses mostly about theory and the intuition of the machine learning concepts. It is okay to not fully memorize the technical details as long as you understand the intuition.
comment_teaching: Prof Nevin explains the concepts well although sometimes I find it lacking and not clear (maybe due to accent issues).
comment_grading: I thin the grading is okay. Most students can get an A based from the other students I asked.
comment_workload:
If you have experience with coding, then the workload can be good. The other questions in the homework about ML concepts can be easily answered.

In order to have good result in the exam, it is important to undestand the intution of the concepts.

author: Sun Tam
comment_content: The material covers fundamental Python topics such as if/else statements, for loops, lists, dictionaries, basic classes, functions/methods, and recursion. It also includes Turtle graphics, a library that lets you create drawings by controlling “turtles” (which act like brushes or pens) through code. The content should be very straightforward for anyone with prior programming experience. However, be cautious with Python’s data types since the language is type-optional, and type hints won’t be taught or tested in exams.
comment_teaching: Alex Lam, the professor, teaches well and often repeats key points to ensure clarity, recognizing that programming concepts can initially be confusing and need reinforcement. That said, I personally found the lectures unnecessary since I already had programming experience and skipped most of them, so I can’t comment much on the teaching overall.
comment_grading:
There are two grading schemes:

24% midterm + 36% labs + 40% final
OR 42% labs + 58% final
The course includes 7 labs plus an introductory lab. Three labs are graded out of 100. The labs themselves are relatively easy, but you must be meticulous because there are usually no partial marks—one small error causing your program to fail a test case can cost you up to 20% of that lab’s score (this actually happened to me). Lab averages typically range from 90 to 100 with a standard deviation of 8 to 10.

The midterm is fairly simple if you’re careful and review your answers. Since the exam is remote, you should have enough time, especially if you have prior programming experience. The midterm average was about 71.9 with a standard deviation around 21.4.

In contrast, the final exam is very challenging. Time is tight, leaving little room to double-check answers. The questions are tricky and sometimes misleading—for example, multiplying a number by 2 when the number is actually a string, not an integer. Extreme caution is required. This semester’s final average was 48.57 with a standard deviation of 19.29.

One strategy is to treat the final as a typing test by quickly typing out the code they don’t allow you to copy. If you can type fast (90-100 words per minute), this approach can reduce stress and improve performance.
comment_workload: The workload is very light. There are 7 labs plus an introductory lab; only 3 labs are submitted and graded. The non-graded labs are optional but worth reviewing since their content often appears on the final exam. The labs are easy as long as you can follow instructions and apply basic programming skills.

author: zlingab
comment_content: The course is well-organized. The topics were presented in a logical order. The course covers a wide range of useful topics.
comment_teaching: The professor is an excellent teacher who does a great job of explaining complex. The classroom atmosphere is very positive, and the professor is approachable and eager to solve the confusion of students.
comment_grading: If you complete all the assignments and projects seriously, you can certainly earn a good grade in this course. These tasks are very effective in helping you develop important skills and concepts. The exam is also manageable if you review the main knowledge carefully.
comment_workload: There are several assignments in this course that require you to do mathematics derive or coding. While these assignments can take some time to complete, they are worth the effort. They are very effective in helping you develop important skills and concepts, and completing them has helped me better understand the course material.

author: FRQ
title: nice
comment_content: Rich and fulfilling. Introduction to major learning paradigms and techniques, basic applied statistics and information theory, decision trees, neural networks, Bayesian classification, kernel methods, clustering, density estimation, feature selection and extraction, hidden Markov models, reinforcement learning, case-based learning, model selection and various applications.
comment_teaching: The teacher explained very clearly, but the pace of the class was a bit tight. More extra effort is needed to better absorb the knowledge learned in class.
comment_grading: nice. The teacher is very kind
comment_workload: The coursework comprises a total of four written homework assignments, one coding assignment, a midterm exam, and a final exam.

author: X-GAO
comment_content: good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good
comment_teaching: good good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good
comment_grading: good good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good
comment_workload: good good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good  good

author: allinmonster
comment_content: I took this course in UG. I would say that this course is the same as what I took. This course will help the student to understand the basic concepts of  machine learning and deep learning. It is very fun to take this course.
comment_teaching:
The professor Nevin L Zhang is nice and give his point accurately;
However, the handwriting is hard to recognize.
comment_grading:
The grading includes 4 assignments and the mid/final exam.
This course only touches on the basics of neural networks-based computer vision. 90% of student can get B+.
comment_workload: Workload is not very high. Totally can handle it. No mid-term, only project and final exam.
